title: Wall-Sit Trophy Hold
description: Stand two feet from a wall and lower into a wall-sit until your knees are bent to roughly 140°. While holding that position, raise your racket arm into the trophy shape — elbow up, racket pointing back. Hold 10 seconds, repeat 5 times. Directly builds the leg-loading habit at the trophy position so the upward drive has something to push off.

title: Slow-Motion Trophy Pause
description: Shadow-serve at 20% speed, stopping completely at the trophy position for a 3-second count before continuing the swing. Use a mirror or record yourself to confirm your knees are visibly bent (target 140°) before you drive upward. 3 sets of 10 shadow serves. The pause trains the nervous system to load the legs rather than skipping straight to the upward swing.

title: Squat-Jump Serve Simulation
description: Without a racket, toss the ball, squat to your trophy position knee angle (target 140°), then explode upward and try to tap the ball at the top of your jump. 3 sets of 8 reps. This isolates the leg-drive component of the serve and teaches you how much power a proper knee bend adds — the jump height difference is immediately noticeable.

title: Trophy Knee Angle Video Check
description: Film your serve from the side at 240fps (slow-motion mode on iPhone). Pause at the trophy frame and visually confirm the angle behind your knee. A straight leg looks like a vertical post; a loaded leg has a clear V-shape behind the knee. Do this check every 2 weeks to track improvement. Awareness of what your actual angle looks like — versus what it feels like — is the fastest path to fixing it.

title: Full-Extension Shadow Swing
description: Stand under a doorframe or at a wall. Shadow-swing your serve and at the contact point try to touch the ceiling or wall with your fingertips fully extended. 3 sets of 15 reps. Grooves the 'reach up and through' sensation at contact so full extension becomes the default rather than a conscious effort.

title: Wall Target Contact Drill
description: Stick a piece of tape on a wall at full-reach height (fingertips extended). Stand racket-length away and practice tapping the tape with your racket strings at the moment of simulated contact. 20 taps per set, 3 sets. Reinforces where your arm needs to be — and confirms full extension is already in your natural swing.

title: Towel Backscratch Drill
description: Hold a small towel in your racket hand and shadow-serve without a racket. The towel should hang down behind your back and brush your spine at the deepest point of the backscratch. 3 sets of 10 swings. The towel gives instant tactile feedback on how deep the racket drop is — if the towel doesn't touch your back, the swing is short.

title: Mirror Backswing Check
description: Stand side-on to a full-length mirror. Shadow-serve slowly and freeze at the deepest point of the backscratch. Check that your elbow is high and pointing toward the sky and the racket tip points toward the ground. 5 freeze-checks per session. Visual confirmation of the drop position is the fastest way to deepen the backscratch without overfitting the feeling.

title: Shelf-Release Toss Drill
description: Place the ball on your open palm as if resting it on a shelf — no fingers curled around it. Practise releasing the ball upward with just a smooth arm lift, no wrist flick. Aim to place the ball at consistent height each time. 30 tosses in a row without a racket. The shelf release eliminates the finger-flick variability that causes toss inconsistency.

title: Fence Toss Consistency Drill
description: Stand next to a fence or wall and toss the ball so it lands on a specific spot on the fence at your target contact height, then catches it on the way down. 20 consecutive tosses — track how many land on the spot. Trains the toss to be a reliable, repeatable motion rather than a new decision on every serve.

title: High-Contact Target Drill
description: Hang a strip of tape or a ribbon from an overhead string at your full-reach contact height. Practise tossing and making contact specifically with that target. 3 sets of 10 serves. Keeps your toss placement dialled in so the ball is always at your natural maximum reach — where you're already making excellent contact.

title: Jump-and-Contact Drill
description: Add a small upward jump at contact to your regular serve to reinforce reaching the highest possible contact point. Focus on timing the jump so you connect with the ball at the peak of both your jump and your arm extension. 2 sets of 8 serves. Bridges knee-loading (leg drive) directly into contact height — the two are connected.

title: Serve Rhythm Counting
description: Assign a count to each phase: '1' = start motion, '2' = trophy position (pause here), '3' = backscratch, '4' = contact. Shadow-serve out loud counting each beat. 3 sets of 10 reps. Rhythm counting forces you to actually hit each position rather than rushing through the motion, and reveals which phase you tend to skip.

title: Serve-and-Shadow
description: Hit one real serve, then immediately shadow-swing the same motion without a ball, focusing on the feel of correct technique. Alternate for 3 sets of 10 serves. The shadow rep after each real serve reinforces the correct movement while the feeling is fresh, building muscle memory faster than blocked repetition alone.

title: Trophy-to-Contact Slow Swing
description: Shadow-serve from trophy position to contact in 5 seconds — deliberately slow. Focus on the transition: legs driving up as the racket drops, then arm extending fully at contact. 4 sets of 8 reps. Isolates the most critical segment of the serve (trophy through contact) and trains each sub-phase sequentially rather than letting momentum carry you through.

title: Shoulder Rotation Towel Swing
description: Hold a towel with both hands at chest height and practise the shoulder turn of the serve: front shoulder points toward the net at the start, then rotates fully so the hitting shoulder drives forward at contact. 3 sets of 20 reps without a racket. Isolates the shoulder coil-and-uncoil that generates rotational power — if the shoulders stay square throughout, you're leaving pace on the serve.

title: Side-On Stance Check
description: Before each serve in practice, pause and check your feet: the front foot should point toward the right net post (for right-handers), and your shoulders should be turned sideways to the net. Take 10 serves focusing only on this setup position. A side-on stance at the start maximises the shoulder rotation available on the way to contact — an open stance shortens the coil.

title: Pronation Coin Drill
description: Hold a coin on the back of your hitting hand. As you swing through the contact zone, rotate your forearm so the coin falls off toward the ground on the follow-through side. 3 sets of 15 shadow swings. This exaggerates the forearm pronation (internal rotation) at contact that snaps the racket head through the ball and generates pace — without it the swing feels like a push rather than a whip.

title: Wrist Snap and Follow-Through
description: Shadow-serve focusing on where the racket ends up after contact: the hitting arm should finish across your body, racket face down, on the opposite hip. If it finishes up in the air, pronation didn't fire. 3 sets of 10 shadow serves, checking the finish position each time. The follow-through position is a reliable readout of whether the wrist snap happened correctly at contact.

title: Toss Placement — Forward Into the Court
description: Place a target (a ball can or towel) on the court one racket-length in front of your front foot. Toss the ball so it would land on that target if you didn't hit it. 20 tosses. A toss that lands behind the baseline forces the contact point back, reducing power and net clearance — a forward toss lets you lean into the ball and drive through it.

title: Toss Arm Straightness Drill
description: Practise the toss with your elbow fully locked straight throughout the entire lifting motion. A bent toss elbow introduces an extra variable that changes the release point and therefore the height. 30 tosses focusing only on keeping the arm rigid. A straight toss arm is one of the simplest ways to make height and placement more repeatable without changing anything else.

title: Follow-Through Finish Position Check
description: After every serve in practice, freeze your finish position for one full second before moving. Check: racket across the body toward the non-dominant hip, hitting shoulder forward, weight on the front foot. 3 sets of 10 serves. The finish position reveals whether the swing path was correct — a serve that ends in the right place almost always had a correct contact too.

title: Split-Step Into Serve Flow
description: Before each practice serve, take one small split-step (a light two-footed hop) to get onto the balls of your feet, then flow directly into the serve motion. 3 sets of 10 serves. The split-step activates the lower body and creates a rhythm trigger at the start of the motion — players who serve flat-footed from a static stance often rush the backswing and arrive at trophy too early.

title: Second Serve Topspin Brush
description: Shadow the second serve swing path: the racket should brush up the back of the ball from 7 o'clock to 1 o'clock (imagining the ball face as a clock). 3 sets of 15 shadow swings, emphasising the upward brushing action rather than driving forward into the ball. A reliable topspin second serve comes from brushing up steeply — a flat swing on the second serve produces double faults under pressure.

title: Target Zone Serving
description: Place three targets (ball cans or cones) in the service box: wide, body, and T positions. Serve 5 balls at each target per set, 3 sets. Call your target out loud before each serve. Directional serving integrates all the technical work done in isolation into a purposeful, game-realistic skill — technique without placement is incomplete.

title: Elbow-Lead Backswing Drill
description: As you initiate the backswing, consciously lead with the elbow rather than the racket head — think 'elbow up first, racket falls after.' Shadow-serve 3 sets of 10, focusing on this sequencing. When the elbow leads, the racket drops naturally into a deep backscratch; when the racket leads, the elbow collapses early and the drop is shallow, costing whip speed at contact.

title: Resistance Band Leg Drive
description: Attach a light resistance band around both thighs just above the knees. Take your serve stance and practise the full trophy-to-contact motion against the band's resistance. 3 sets of 8 serves. The band forces you to push outward with the knees to maintain position, which activates the glutes and hip abductors that are often inactive in a straight-legged serve. Over time this builds the strength to maintain a loaded position comfortably.
